auth_module_map.role: Defines AbstractBaseUser (line 43) and BaseUserManager (line 21). AbstractBaseUser provides only password (line 44) and last_login (line 45) as concrete fields, plus authentication protocol methods (set_password, check_password, get_session_auth_hash). This is the minimal base for custom user models.

Defines PermissionsMixin (line 317) with is_superuser (line 323), groups (line 331), and user_permissions (line 342). Defines AbstractUser (line 446) extending AbstractBaseUser + PermissionsMixin with: username (line 456), first_name (line 468), last_name (line 469), email (line 470), is_staff (line 471), is_active (line 476), date_joined (line 484). Also defines UserManager (line 140) with create_user/create_superuser helpers.
Initial migration creating Permission, Group, and User tables. Custom user app migrations must declare a dependency on this file when swapping AUTH_USER_MODEL and using PermissionsMixin.
Migration that made last_login nullable — relevant because AbstractBaseUser.last_login (base_user.py:45) is null=True and custom models inheriting it must keep that nullable contract.
Exports get_user_model(), the integration point for referencing the swappable user model. All code using User directly must migrate to get_user_model() or settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL.

migration_trace: Step 1 — Audit AbstractUser vs AbstractBaseUser: AbstractBaseUser (base_user.py:43) provides only password (base_user.py:44) and last_login (base_user.py:45). All other AbstractUser fields — username (models.py:456), first_name (models.py:468), last_name (models.py:469), email (models.py:470), is_staff (models.py:471), is_active (models.py:476), date_joined (models.py:484) — plus PermissionsMixin fields is_superuser (models.py:323), groups (models.py:331), user_permissions (models.py:342) — must be explicitly re-declared on the new model.
Step 2 — Set AUTH_USER_MODEL = 'myapp.User' in settings.py before any migration runs (required by Django's swappable model system; see AbstractUser.Meta.swappable at models.py:446).

Step 3 — Rewrite the custom User model to extend AbstractBaseUser (base_user.py:43) instead of AbstractUser (models.py:446). Set USERNAME_FIELD and REQUIRED_FIELDS class attributes (protocol required by AbstractBaseUser, base_user.py:49). Re-declare all needed AbstractUser fields verbatim. Include PermissionsMixin (models.py:317) if group/permission support is needed.
Step 4 — Implement a custom UserManager extending BaseUserManager (base_user.py:21) with create_user() and create_superuser(), following the pattern at models.py:158-210.
Step 5 — Retain all three custom fields (profile_tier, external_sso_id, last_sync_at) as explicit declarations on the new model; none exist anywhere in contrib.auth.

Step 6 — Run 'python manage.py makemigrations'. If PermissionsMixin is used, the generated migration must depend on 'auth.0001_initial' (migrations/0001_initial.py:7-10) because Permission and Group models are created there.

Step 7 — If the database has existing data, write a RunPython data migration to copy rows from the old user table to the new schema, preserving all three custom field values and re-declared AbstractUser fields.
Step 8 — Update all FK/M2M references to the old User model across admin.py, forms, and backends to use get_user_model() (auth/__init__.py) rather than direct model imports.

unmappable_fields.reason: No tier or subscription-level concept exists anywhere in AbstractBaseUser (base_user.py:43-164) or AbstractUser (models.py:446-514). contrib.auth models identity and authentication only; business-tier classification is application-domain data with no auth equivalent.
unmappable_fields.migration_strategy: Declare profile_tier = models.CharField(max_length=20, choices=[('free','Free'),('pro','Pro'),('enterprise','Enterprise')]) directly on the new AbstractBaseUser subclass. The field already exists in the database so makemigrations produces no ALTER TABLE. No data migration needed.
unmappable_fields.evidence: django/contrib/auth/models.py:446 (AbstractUser — field absent); django/contrib/auth/base_user.py:43 (AbstractBaseUser — field absent)

unmappable_fields.reason: contrib.auth has no SSO identifier field. AbstractUser's username (models.py:456) is the closest identity anchor but carries Django's own UnicodeUsernameValidator and uniqueness semantics that conflict with an opaque SSO ID. external_sso_id is nullable+unique — a distinct concept from username.
unmappable_fields.migration_strategy: Declare external_sso_id = models.CharField(max_length=255, unique=True, null=True, blank=True) on the new model. Do NOT map it to username — keep both. Add a custom authentication backend (following the interface in django/contrib/auth/backends.py) that looks up users by external_sso_id when the SSO provider posts it.
unmappable_fields.evidence: django/contrib/auth/models.py:456 (username field — different semantics and validation); django/contrib/auth/base_user.py:43 (AbstractBaseUser — no SSO field)

unmappable_fields.reason: The only timestamp in contrib.auth is last_login (base_user.py:45), updated automatically by the update_last_login signal receiver (models.py:18-24). last_sync_at tracks an external data-sync event, not a login event — semantically distinct and not writable by Django's auth machinery.
unmappable_fields.migration_strategy: Declare last_sync_at = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True) on the new model. Do NOT conflate with last_login (base_user.py:45). Update it explicitly in the service layer that performs the external sync via User.objects.filter(pk=pk).update(last_sync_at=timezone.now()). The field already exists in the database; no structural migration needed.
unmappable_fields.evidence: django/contrib/auth/base_user.py:45 (last_login — login-specific only); django/contrib/auth/models.py:18-24 (update_last_login signal — updates last_login only, no sync concept)
